research_summary: Atlan is the 'Context Layer for AI' -- infrastructure that encodes enterprise business context (definitions, policies, lineage) so AI agents reason correctly at inference time. $105M Series C, 400+ enterprise customers (GM, Nasdaq, Workday). Strong India engineering team. No specific AI Engineer role open but EM Data Platform JD shows exactly what they value: multi-tenant retrieval, AI-native metadata, high-scale ingestion, sub-second search. Amit's permission-aware RAG with Milvus 2-index architecture and 250+ permissions per query is a direct technical match. 6 contacts captured: 2 Tier 1 (EM/senior), 2 Tier 3 (IC), 2 Tier 4 (recruiter).
jd_full_text:
Engineering Manager, Data Platform -- India Remote

Who We Are
Most companies are racing to deploy AI, but very few have the foundation to make it work reliably. Atlan is building that missing layer: the context layer for enterprise AI. We connect the business context behind data so humans and agents can operate with far more accuracy and confidence.

With backing from world-class investors including GIC, Insight Partners, Meritech, Peak XV, and Salesforce Ventures, we've earned the trust of most AI-forward enterprises like General Motors, Nasdaq, Workday and Elastic.

The Opportunity
Atlan is building the context layer for the AI era, the control plane that makes enterprise data usable, governed, extensible, and AI-ready.

We're looking for an Engineering Manager to join one of our core platform teams. This is not a coordination role. We need an engineer-first, hands-on platform leader who has built and owned foundational systems at scale, whether that's distributed platform primitives and ingestion infrastructure, or data quality systems and AI-native product surfaces that sit on top of them.

Depending on the team you join, you'll own one of two high-leverage problem spaces:

Data Quality & Context Intelligence - proactive detection and resolution of data issues (schema drift, freshness, anomalies) at scale; and using AI to auto-generate metadata context (descriptions, classifications, lineage) for thousands of undocumented enterprise data assets.

App Platform - the shared runtime, SDK, lifecycle, and distribution infrastructure that powers every app, agent, and workflow built on Atlan's platform.

What You'll Do

Build the AI-ready context foundation
Lead the engineering charter to design and evolve an AI-ready context store across structured, unstructured, vector, and graph systems. This platform powers apps, agents, and enterprise workflows on top of trusted data.

Own core platform primitives
Drive end-to-end delivery of complex platform capabilities, including:
- High-scale ingestion and validation frameworks
- Contract-driven routing, storage, and serving layers
- Multi-tenant isolation and noisy-neighbor resilience
- Search APIs with sub-second P95 response times
- Systems operating at 99.99% availability

Lead from the front
Engage deeply in architecture and systems design. Guide distributed systems tradeoffs, review RFCs, unblock critical issues, and participate meaningfully in technical decisions. This role requires technical depth, not just coordination.

Scale teams and operational excellence
Build and mentor high-performing engineers and managers. Define engineering metrics, on-call practices, SLO discipline, and incident processes. Strengthen the operational engine behind a platform serving 100K+ users and billions of assets.

What Great Looks Like

Experience building data or platform primitives at scale:
- Data ingestion frameworks or lakehouse foundations
- Metadata, governance, or catalog platforms
- Multi-tenant SaaS infrastructure and tenant isolation mechanisms
- Search, storage, or distributed serving layers
- Data quality, validation, or contract enforcement systems

Platform leadership experience: led and scaled engineering teams, ideally within platform, infrastructure, or data-heavy SaaS organizations.

Strong distributed systems and reliability depth: credibility in distributed systems, cloud-native architectures, and operating high-availability platforms with strict SLOs.

Builder mindset with high agency: thrive in ambiguity, move fast with ownership, build foundational systems from first principles.

AI-native curiosity: actively explore AI-assisted development workflows and think critically about how agentic systems will reshape developer productivity and platform design.
tailoring_notes: 1. Professional Summary rewritten to mirror Atlan's 'context layer' and 'retrieval and context delivery' language. Proof points front-loaded: Milvus 2-index architecture, 250+ permissions per query, 50K+ queries/month, 95% MRR, GPT-4o/Gemini/Qwen3-Coder orchestration. Dropped generic 'AI Engineer' framing in favor of 'context delivery systems'. 2. Technical Skills reordered: Vector DBs and Retrieval moved to first position (core to Atlan's context store product), then LLMs and AI Frameworks (combined LLM + LangChain), then Languages, then Infrastructure. Hardware/IoT bullet dropped entirely -- not relevant to AI platform role. 3. All changes align with JD signals: multi-tenant isolation, sub-second search, high-scale retrieval, AI-native metadata generation, embedding benchmarking.

contacts.contact_role: Senior Engineering Manager, AI-native context engineering
contacts.research_hook: 3 weeks ago ran a free workshop 'From Agent Demo to Agent System' where he argued agent failures are infrastructure problems (not model problems): 'it invents policies that don't exist, forgets customers between sessions, burns money looping.' He covered all nine components of a production agent framework including context engineering and memory. Amit's permission-aware LangGraph pipeline at Vivansh is exactly this -- a context layer that enforces 250+ policies per query before any retrieval happens.
contacts.subject: your agent architecture workshop
contacts.content:
Caught your workshop a few weeks ago -- "From Agent Demo to Agent System." The bit about agents inventing policies that don't exist landed close to home. That's exactly what our EHS chatbot was doing before we rebuilt the retrieval layer.

What we ended up with: a 2-stage Milvus pipeline where the first index is just permissions. 250+ access rules resolved before any retrieval happens, cross-referenced with PostgreSQL at query time. LangGraph handles the routing. It's running at 500+ concurrent users and about 1,000 queries/day without hallucinating policy.

I'm two years into production AI -- the 50K queries/month RAG rebuild at Silver Touch (MRR from 45% to 95%), an on-prem OCR pipeline for government docs, and the EHS system above. I've been tracking Atlan since the Activate launch and the Context Agents demo caught my attention (8% to 100% metadata coverage is a real number).

If your team has any interest in someone who has shipped this kind of infrastructure, I'd welcome 15 minutes. If the timing's off, no worries.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Bumping this in case it got buried.

One thing I left out: the embedding benchmarking behind the retrieval layer. Tested BGE-M3, BGE-small/base/large, Stella, and SPLADE on production data before settling on BGE-M3 with a reranker. The latency tradeoff didn't resolve until we layered intent classification on top. Figured that was more relevant given your work on context engineering.

If now's not the right time, totally fine.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Last note from me. If Atlan isn't actively hiring for AI engineering right now, I get it -- would still appreciate being on your radar when it comes up.

Either way, good luck with the next WTF is the Context Layer episode.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai

contacts.research_hook: 2 weeks ago posted 'Most AI models are built to be capable. The best ones are built to be purposeful.' -- discussing how Anthropic built Claude for reliability over benchmarks. He is also writing 'Agentic AI Systems' book while building third-gen data/AI platform at Atlan. Ex-CTO background means he thinks about architecture at the full-system level.
contacts.subject: on purposeful vs capable -- production angle
contacts.content:
Read your post from a couple of weeks ago about Anthropic and purposeful AI. The framing resonated -- "built for benchmark scores vs built for reliability" is the exact tension I've run into rebuilding RAG systems in production.

Quick context on why I'm writing: I've spent the last two years shipping AI that has to work reliably, not just score well. A multi-tenant RAG chatbot at 50K+ queries/month (MRR from 45% to 95% after I rebuilt the retrieval layer), a permission-aware LangGraph pipeline enforcing 250+ access rules per query, a multi-model orchestration pipeline running GPT-4o + Qwen3-Coder + Gemini on one flow. The production-first constraint shapes every architecture decision.

I've been following Atlan since the Activate launch and I'm working through your "Agentic AI Systems" material. The context store problem -- how you deliver trusted enterprise context to agents at inference time -- maps directly to what I've been building, just at the enterprise data layer rather than the EHS domain.

If you'd be open to 15 minutes, I'd like to understand how you think about the retrieval architecture behind the context store. Not asking for secrets, just curious about the tradeoffs you're navigating.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: on purposeful vs capable -- production angle
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Following up in case this got buried.

One more specific detail worth adding: the on-prem OCR pipeline I built at Silver Touch processed 200+ page government PDFs for sensitive docs (Aadhaar, PAN, GST). Used DocTR + DocLink with Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70b, got to about 90% entity extraction accuracy. The constraint was that no data could leave the building. Different flavor of the context delivery problem -- trusted extraction under hard access restrictions.

If now's not the right time, I'm fine circling back.

Amit

contacts.contact_role: Senior Software Engineer, AI Observability & LLM-native systems
contacts.research_hook: IIT Madras grad, 3x GSoC mentor, building AI observability and LLM-native systems at Atlan. Recently shared Atlan's Software Engineer Intern posting and engaged with DuckDB-skills launch. His work on LLM observability pipelines is exactly where Amit's multi-model orchestration experience (GPT-4o + Qwen3-Coder + Gemini on one flow, each stage validated with Pydantic) becomes directly relevant.
contacts.subject: LLM observability + multi-model orchestration
contacts.content:
Saw you're building LLM observability pipelines at Atlan. That's the exact layer I've been wishing we had better tooling for at Vivansh.

We run a multi-model flow: GPT-4o for access control and query rewriting, Qwen3-Coder-480B for code-heavy generation, GPT-4o-mini for cheaper classification passes. Each stage is Pydantic-validated with a schema contract. Without proper observability you're flying blind on which model is failing which query type -- we ended up logging at every stage and inspecting by hand, which doesn't scale.

I'm two years into production AI (RAG at 50K queries/month, the multi-model pipeline above, an on-prem OCR system for government docs) and I've been watching Atlan's work since the Activate launch. The Context Agents demo in particular -- the jump from 8% to 100% metadata coverage using AI agents is the kind of production result that takes real infrastructure behind it.

If your team is hiring or planning to hire on the AI side, I'd be curious to hear more about the observability stack you're building. Happy to share notes on the multi-model orchestration setup too.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Bumping this in case it got lost.

One more thing worth adding: the 3x GSoC mentoring caught my attention. I didn't do GSoC but I did a research internship at IIT Delhi in early 2024 -- air writing recognition with ESP32 + CNN-BiLSTM, 75% user-dependent accuracy across 62 alphanumeric characters. I mention it because the open-source and research angle of your work is something I find genuinely interesting, not just the production side.

If the timing isn't right, no worries.

Amit

I know you sometimes offer guidance to people asking about Atlan. I'm not asking for a referral or anything like that, just whether your experience of the engineering culture matches what I'm reading from the outside.
